title: Fixing Type Inference for a Generic Function

statement.inputFormat: Two objects of possibly different types
statement.outputFormat: A new object that is the merge of the two input objects
statement.notes: The `merge` function should not modify the original objects.

examples.input: const obj1 = { a: 1, b: 2 }; const obj2 = { b: 3, c: 4 };
examples.explanation: Merge two objects with different keys

examples.input: const obj1 = { a: 1, b: 2 }; const obj2 = { a: 3, c: 4 };
examples.explanation: Merge two objects with the same key

examples.input: const obj1 = { a: 1 }; const obj2 = { b: 2, c: 3 };
examples.explanation: Merge two objects with different keys

hints.text: Use the spread operator to merge the two objects

editorial.title: Editorial Solution
editorial.intuition: The `merge` function should use the spread operator to merge the two objects. The spread operator will automatically override any duplicate keys with the values from the second object.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute force solution is to manually check each key in the first object and add it to the result object. Then, manually check each key in the second object and add it to the result object if it doesn't exist yet.
editorial.betterSolution: Use the spread operator to merge the two objects
editorial.optimalSolution: Use the spread operator to merge the two objects
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The spread operator will automatically override any duplicate keys with the values from the second object.
editorial.dryRun: Merge two objects using the spread operator

functionSignature.javascript: function merge<T, U>(obj1: T, obj2: U) {
functionSignature.python: def merge(self, obj1: dict, obj2: dict) -> dict:

testCases.input: const obj1 = { a: 1, b: 2 }; const obj2 = { b: 3, c: 4 };
const obj1 = { a: 1, b: 2 }; const obj2 = { a: 3, c: 4 };
const obj1 = { a: 1 }; const obj2 = { b: 2, c: 3 };
